nativeRange.habitat: Found across a range of habitat types, from small upland headwater streams to main river channels and lakes. In all of these settings it tends to associate with submerged tree branches, roots, and floating logs, and it migrates into seasonally flooded areas during the wet season.

decorPreferences: sand or fine smooth-gravel substrate to protect barbels
water-worn rocks and smooth boulders arranged to form caves and crevices
bogwood and driftwood providing hiding spots and visual barriers
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria) to reduce line-of-sight aggression
avoid sharp-edged decorations that can trap sub-ocular spines

diet.notes: Though classified as an omnivore, the Tiger Loach is primarily carnivorous in practice. It readily accepts quality sinking pellets alongside live and frozen foods such as bloodworms, Tubifex, brine shrimp (Artemia), white mosquito larvae, chopped prawns, and mysis shrimp. It also takes fresh vegetables including cucumber, courgette, melon, and spinach, and will actively hunt and consume aquatic snails. Small portions fed once or twice daily are recommended, and offering a varied diet is important for long-term health.

goodWith: large fast-moving cyprinids (Devario, Danio, Rasbora species)
other large botiid loaches of similar size (Syncrossus helodes, Yasuhikotakia morleti)
avoidWith: small or slow-moving fish that may be intimidated or predated
long-finned or delicate fish (bettas, fancy guppies, angelfish)
fish smaller than approximately 6–8 cm that may be eaten

summary: The Tiger Loach (Syncrossus hymenophysa) is a large, boldly striped loach from Sumatra and Borneo that grows to around 21 cm and ranks among the most aggressive members of its family. It requires a group of at least five in a spacious, well-established aquarium and is not suitable for peaceful community tanks.
careNotes: The Tiger Loach is one of the larger and more aggressive members of the family Botiidae, and represents a significant step up from the more commonly kept clown loach or yoyo loach. The genus Syncrossus was separated from Botia in 2004, and older trade literature still frequently uses the earlier name Botia hymenophysa. Adults reach 21 cm in standard length and require a tank of at least 150 cm base length and approximately 300 litres or more. A group of at least 5–6 individuals — preferably 8 or more — is essential: singly kept specimens tend to become withdrawn, refuse food, or direct aggression toward all tankmates, while a sufficiently large group allows a stable dominance hierarchy to form and distributes aggression among the fish themselves. Unlike most loaches, the Tiger Loach is genuinely aggressive rather than merely boisterous — it will actively intimidate, chase, and injure smaller or slower tankmates. Suitable companions are large, fast-swimming cyprinids (Devario, Danio, larger Rasbora) and robust barbs that can hold their own; long-finned and slow-moving fish should be avoided entirely. Water quality is critical: the Tiger Loach is highly intolerant of organic waste accumulation and should never be introduced to biologically immature aquaria. Strong filtration providing roughly 4–5 times the tank volume in turnover per hour, good oxygenation, and weekly water changes of 30–50% are all recommended. Preferred parameters are soft to moderately hard water (gH/kH below 12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.0–7.5), and a temperature of 25–30°C, reflecting the species' Sumatran and Bornean riverine origin. Like all botiids, the Tiger Loach possesses sharp, retractable sub-ocular spines (small defensive spines located just below the eye) that can tangle fatally in nets; smooth containers or bags should be used for transfers, and sharp tank decorations should be avoided. The species is susceptible to "skinny disease" caused by flagellate internal parasites (Spironucleus, Hexamita) and responds poorly to copper-based medications, which must be avoided entirely. A sand substrate of at least 3 cm depth helps protect the sensory barbels. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory, as this species is capable of jumping.
breedingNotes: The Tiger Loach has not been bred in captivity. In the wild it is presumed to be a seasonal, migratory spawner, likely triggered by wet-season flooding and accompanying shifts in water chemistry. Females are noticeably stockier than males and are believed to grow somewhat larger, though reliable sexing outside of this difference is not documented. No captive spawning protocol exists in the hobby literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Syncrossus hymenophysa
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (150–210 mm SL), temperature (25–30°C), pH (5.0–7.5), hardness (18–215 ppm / approximately 0–12 dH), minimum tank dimensions (150 × 45 cm, ~304 L), diet, native range (Sumatra, Sarawak, West Kalimantan), habitat description, group size (5–6+), aggression notes, breeding status, and husbandry constraints including copper sensitivity and sub-ocular spines.

additional.name: FishBase — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (21.0 cm SL), native range (Indonesia: Sumatra and Borneo; Malaya), habitat (freshwater demersal; lakes, rivers, streams near submerged wood), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes). Confirms FishBase max size aligns with SeriouslyFish upper bound.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Confirmed adult size (25 cm TL in trade), temperature (25–30°C), pH (7.0 or below), hardness (<12 dH), minimum tank length (4 ft+), diet (sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, chopped prawns), group size (5+ minimum), aggression profile, substrate and hiding-place requirements. Also noted confusion with S. helodes and stripe-pattern distinction.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Tiger Botia Loach (Syncrossus hymenophysa)
additional.notes: Supplemental source. Confirmed pH (6.0–7.0), KH 8–12, gH <12, minimum 55-gallon (208 L) tank, lifespan ~7 years, group of 5+ same-size individuals, diet (bloodworms, worms, larvae), semi-aggressive temperament, no captive breeding records. Lists Java (historically) and Musi River basin, Sarawak, West Kalimantan, Pahang/Perak river systems in range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Syncrossus hymenophysa
additional.notes: Used to confirm genus-level taxonomy split (Syncrossus separated from Botia by Kottelat 2004), alternate common name Green Tiger Loach, distribution (Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo, Sumatra), and general size up to 10 in (25 cm). Also confirmed Least Concern IUCN status.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been recorded. The species is presumed to spawn seasonally in the wild, likely migrating to flooded areas triggered by wet-season conditions. Females are noticeably stockier and are believed to be somewhat larger than males. No aquarium breeding protocol exists.
